original_abstract:
Members of the solute carrier organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP) family are essential transporters for a diverse array of amphipathic organic anions, ranging from endogenous metabolites like bile salts and steroids to critical clinical drugs such as statins and anticancer agents. Among these, OATP1B1 is a key player in hepatic clearance, mediating the uptake of substances into the liver. Due to its central role in drug disposition, both the FDA and EMA recommend rigorous in vitro testing for OATP1B1-mediated drug–drug interactions (DDIs). However, despite its physiological and clinical importance, the structural basis and transport mechanisms of the OATP family have remained poorly understood. 

In this study, we present various cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 in both outward-open and inward-open states. These structures were determined in complex with diverse ligands, including endogenous metabolites (bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate), the antiviral drug simeprevir, and the fluorescent probe 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein. Our findings reveal the existence of distinct major and minor substrate-binding pockets and define the conformational transitions that occur during the transport cycle. By integrating structural data with mutagenesis studies and molecular dynamics simulations, we provide a comprehensive framework for the transport mechanism of OATP1B1. This work offers a structural foundation for predicting DDIs and significantly advances our fundamental understanding of the OATP transporter family.
humanized_abstract:
In mammals, the solute carrier organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP) family constitutes an important group of transporters responsible for the cellular entry of a broad variety of amphipathic organic anions. These include physiologically occurring molecules like bile salts and steroid derivatives as well as clinical drugs such as statins and many anticancer drugs. The distribution and functional role of the hepatic transporter OATP1B1 has drawn great attention, especially since the FDA and EMA advise the performance of detailed in vitro interaction studies with potential OATP1B1 substrates and inhibitors. Despite its well-recognized functional role, relatively little is presently known about the structural properties of OATP proteins and their mode of action.

We report cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 transport protein in both outward-open and inward-open states, in association with several ligands including native substrates (bilirubin, estrone-3-sulfate) and a drug (simeprevir), as well as a dye (2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein). The characterization of various ligand interactions and major and minor substrate-binding sites in OATP1B1 as well as the mapping of the substrate-induced transport-related conformational changes complemented by mutagenesis and MD studies establish the molecular transport mechanism for OATP1B1 and provide a solid basis for advancing our understanding of the overall OATP transporter family. Our data will also be instrumental in improving predictions of DDI.
